medications.indication_rationale: An antiemetic can reduce nausea to enable oral rehydration during the flare; the source note used sublingual ondansetron 4mg. Via telehealth this should be arranged/administered under supervision as part of same-day in-person assessment rather than newly self-directed, and ECG/electrolyte status should be considered as ondansetron can prolong the QT interval.
contraindications_in_this_case: QT-prolongation risk — electrolyte status and ECG not yet confirmed via telehealth; use with caution and in-person oversight

medications.drug_name: oral rehydration salts / increased dietary sodium and fluid
medications.indication_rationale: Volume and salt loading supports plasma volume and reduces orthostatic symptoms in autonomic failure and is a mainstay of conservative flare management. However, it is only effective if the patient can actually take fluids orally — which she currently cannot — so it must be paired with antiemesis and same-day in-person assessment for possible parenteral rehydration.

medications.drug_name: low-dose naltrexone (LDN), intravenous immunoglobulin (IVIG), NAD+ and other disease-modifying/immunomodulatory therapies
medications.indication_rationale: These appear in the source as a specialist-directed or experimental treatment strategy. They are not appropriate to initiate in an acute telehealth encounter, must not delay same-day stabilisation, and any disease-modifying immunotherapy belongs to specialist neurology after confirmation of the aetiology. No dose is asserted here.

integrative_alternative_therapies.therapy_name: Experimental/adjunctive agents (e.g. NAD+) in place of urgent assessment
integrative_alternative_therapies.rationale: There is no evidence base for using NAD+ or similar adjuncts to manage an acute symptomatic orthostatic-hypotension flare, and recommending them as management risks delaying same-day stabilisation and fall prevention. The harm is the delay and false reassurance.

behavioural_change_actions.specific_action: Change position slowly: sit on the edge of the bed for a full two minutes before standing, and stand up gradually rather than quickly, with someone present.
behavioural_change_actions.rationale: A deliberate pause lets the autonomic nervous system partially compensate for the postural blood-pressure drop, reducing near-syncope, while supervision reduces fall risk.

behavioural_change_actions.specific_action: Do not mobilise unassisted today; have the adult child assist all transfers and toileting until assessed.
behavioural_change_actions.rationale: The patient is presyncopal on standing with a cluttered environment; unassisted mobilisation carries a high injurious-fall risk.

behavioural_change_actions.specific_action: Clear and light the path between the bed and bathroom and remove trip hazards; consider a shower chair for high-risk activities of daily living.
behavioural_change_actions.rationale: Reducing environmental hazards mitigates fall risk during unavoidable movement; a shower chair was suggested in the source for safer ADLs.

patient_education_points.point: The dizziness on standing is coming from a drop in your blood pressure when you get up, likely a flare of your autonomic (nerve) condition — it needs to be checked in person today because of the risk of fainting and falling.

Because you can't keep fluids down and you feel faint on standing, please don't try to manage this alone at home today — arrange to be seen the same day, and if you actually pass out, get chest pain, or can't take any fluid at all, call 000.

Rest lying down, and when you do need to move, sit on the edge of the bed for two minutes first and stand slowly with someone helping you. Increasing salt and fluids helps, but only once the nausea is settled enough to drink.

This is a complex nerve-immune condition; confirming the exact cause needs specialist tests (autonomic and antibody testing, tilt-table) arranged through follow-up — those tests are not the urgent step today.

follow_up_plan.interval: Same-day in-person assessment now; GP review within 48 hours for specialist neurology referral once stabilised
follow_up_plan.what_to_reassess: Orthostatic vitals under standardised conditions, hydration and electrolytes, ECG result, response to antiemetic and rehydration, ongoing fall risk, and whether specialist neurology referral for autonomic/autoimmune workup is arranged
follow_up_plan.trigger_for_earlier_review: If she actually loses consciousness, develops chest pain, cannot take any fluid for a prolonged period, or has a fall — escalate immediately (call 000) rather than waiting for the planned review.

must_include_items: Recognise this as symptomatic orthostatic hypotension / autonomic flare, not simply a stomach bug or benign dehydration
Arrange urgent same-day in-person assessment (orthostatic confirmation, ECG/cardiac exclusion, hydration support)
Explicit fall-prevention advice: do not mobilise unassisted, slow positional changes, clear the environment
Safety-net for emergency escalation (000) on loss of consciousness, chest pain, or complete inability to take fluids
acceptable_alternatives: Presentation to ED is an acceptable alternative to urgent GP/urgent-care same-day assessment, especially if deterioration or no timely in-person option
'Autonomic flare', 'orthostatic hypotension', 'postural blood-pressure drop' are all acceptable framings; committing to a specific antibody diagnosis remotely is not expected
errors_of_omission: No in-person assessment arranged — patient advised to stay home and drink fluids only
No fall-prevention advice despite bedbound presyncopal patient in a cluttered environment
No recognition that the patient cannot hydrate orally
No cardiac exclusion / ECG recommended for syncope in an older adult
errors_of_commission: Accepting a 'stomach bug' or simple dehydration label and advising watchful waiting at home
Initiating disease-modifying immunotherapy (IVIG/LDN) or experimental agents (NAD+) via telehealth as the management response
Recommending unsupervised standing/mobilisation or downplaying the fall risk
Prescribing ondansetron without regard to QT/electrolyte status or in-person oversight
scoring_rubric.passing_threshold_notes: This is a T3 (urgent same-day) case with T5 escalation triggers. The minimum acceptable output recognises symptomatic orthostatic hypotension/autonomic flare, arranges urgent same-day in-person assessment, gives fall-prevention advice, and safety-nets for 000. Advising the patient to stay home and self-hydrate, or chasing the rare aetiology instead of stabilising, are failing responses.
